name: Hejazi Arabic
notes: Hejazi Arabic (حجازي), urban western Saudi Arabia: Mecca, Medina, Jeddah. References Omar (1975) and Abdoh (2010). DEFINING FEATURES modeled here: (1) qaf ق → /ɡ/ (voiced velar stop) in inherited vocabulary — part of the early Hejazi /q/→/ɡ/ shift; /q/ retained only in learned/scientific/religious borrowings (older descriptions positing a /ʔ/ reflex are a misattribution of the Levantine/Egyptian feature); (2) jim ج → /dʒ/ (affricate; [ʒ] for some speakers); (3) interdentals MERGED TO STOPS in the urban koine — ث → /t/ (~/s/), ذ → /d/ (~/z/), ظ → /dˤ/ (~/zˤ/); interdentals are retained only in Bedouin/rural speech; (4) MONOPHTHONGIZATION — Classical /aj aw/ → [eː oː] (bayt→beːt, yawm→joːm), declared as allophone_rules HEJ_MONO_* (Omar 1975; Abdoh 2010), giving the 8-vowel system /a u i; aː uː iː eː oː/. TOKENIZATION NOTE: those rules rewrite the /aj/ and /aw/ ATOMS that the ⟨َي⟩/⟨َو⟩ digraphs emit, and in ⟨يَوْ⟩ and ⟨وَيْ⟩ the fatha is already spoken for — the inherited `ar` onset digraphs يَ→/ja/ and وَ→/wa/ claim it, so the glide that follows is a bare /w/ or /j/ and the diphthong atom never forms (يَوْم came out *[jawm]). The two sequences are therefore spelled out in `graphemes` as يَوْ→[joː] and وَيْ→[weː], which is the same monophthongization, reached from the spelling instead of from the atom. The explicit sukun in both keys is what keeps حَيَوَان /ħajawaːn/ and يَوَدّ /jawadd/ — where the waw carries its own vowel and there is no diphthong — out of the rule; (5) four emphatics /sˤ dˤ tˤ zˤ/ with pharyngealization spreading that backs low vowels — inherited from the ar-x-peninsular parent (AR_PEN_EMPH_*). No k→[tʃ] Gulf/Najdi affrication. Cosmopolitan koine shaped by Hajj contact. INPUT CONTRACT: transcribes orthography+harakat; short vowels surface only when the input is vocalised (dialectal Arabic is normally written defectively). NO ar-SA UMBRELLA NODE: Saudi dialects do not form one phonological group — they split across the Hejazi (this node), Najdi (ar-SA-x-najd + ar-SA-x-qassim), Gulf (ar-SA-x-sharqiyya) and Southwestern (ar-SA-x-rijal-alma) types — so the bare country code ar-SA is left unassigned (lang-code accuracy: null beats wrong; Omar 1975). The urban Hejazi koine modeled here is itself only the prestige city variety; rural and Bedouin Hejazi retain interdentals and lack the stop mergers.

sources.author: Omar, M.K.
sources.title: Saudi Arabic, Urban Hijazi Dialect: A Basic Course
sources.notes: Urban Hijazi phonemic inventory: qaf→/ɡ/, jim→/dʒ/, interdentals merged to stops, 8-vowel system, monophthongization of /aj aw/ → /eː oː/.

sources.author: Abdoh, E.
sources.title: A Study of the Phonology and Morphology of Urban Meccan Arabic (PhD dissertation)
sources.notes: Urban Meccan/Hejazi vowel chart; 8-vowel system with /eː oː/ from monophthongized diphthongs; four emphatics /sˤ dˤ tˤ zˤ/.

sources.author: Al Mahmoud, M.S.
sources.title: A Constraint-based Analysis of Velar Affrication in Najdi vs. Hijazi Arabic
sources.notes: Native-informant evidence that Hijazi has NO velar affrication: /kis/, /dik/, /ʕɪlk/, /keɪf/, /kɛlb/ all keep [k] in HA while Najdi alternates with [ts]; the qaf reflex is [ɡ] with no [dz]. Supports the k:[k] allophone list here.

sources.author: Watson, J.C.E.
sources.title: The Phonology and Morphology of Arabic

sources.author: Holes, C.
sources.title: Modern Arabic: Structures, Functions, and Varieties

allophone_rules.notes: Urban Hejazi monophthongization (Omar 1975; Abdoh 2010): the Classical diphthong /aj/ is realised [eː], e.g. bayt → [beːt]. Yields the 8-vowel system /a u i aː uː iː eː oː/.
Urban Hejazi monophthongization (Omar 1975; Abdoh 2010): the Classical diphthong /aw/ is realised [oː], e.g. yawm → [joːm].

location.notes: Glottolog's representative point for Hijazi Arabic.

stress.notes: Arabic stress is QUANTITY-SENSITIVE: it falls on a syllable because that syllable is heavy, not because of any orthographic ending, so no ending table can express it. The cascade (Ryding, A Reference Grammar of MSA, CUP 2005, §2.3; Watson, The Phonology and Morphology of Arabic, OUP 2002, ch.3): a superheavy final syllable (CVːC/CVCC) takes the stress (kiˈtaːb); otherwise a heavy penult (CVː/CVC) does (muˈdarris); otherwise the antepenult (ˈmadrasa). max_onset=1 because Arabic onsets are obligatory and hold exactly one consonant — a medial cluster splits, closing the preceding syllable, and that boundary is what makes the penult of mudarris heavy. INPUT CONTRACT: weight is only recoverable from a complete transcription, so this requires diacritized input; an undiacritized skeleton has no vowels to weigh. The registered wikipron gold carries no stress marks, but the benchmark strips stress from both sides by default, so this costs no PER. Prosodic clitics: monosyllabic function words (negator maː, prepositions fiː/min/ʕan/ʕa/li/bi, vocative jaː, copula-negator muː/miʃ, conditional law/loː, wa-proclitic combinations) attach to a host and fall inside the host's stress domain, so they carry no word stress (Watson 2002, The Phonology and Morphology of Arabic, OUP, ch.3). Pan-Arabic class; the per-lect form list reflects the function words attested in this lect. Orthographic test — a clitic homograph (e.g. verb yā 'came') is destressed in every occurrence.
